lead: Preparation is the secret behind every calm long-distance move, and it begins long before the van turns up. Over twenty years of moving families out of Suffolk to far-flung corners of the country, we've learned that the work you do in the weeks beforehand decides whether the day feels controlled or chaotic. Here is the practical, first-hand checklist our crews wish every customer had, set out in the order things actually need doing.

sections.h: Begin Six to Eight Weeks Out
sections.body:
A long-distance move needs a longer runway than a local one, and we always encourage people to start thinking about it a good two months ahead. The single most useful thing you can do early is decide what is genuinely coming with you. When your belongings are about to travel hundreds of miles, it makes no sense to pay to move things you do not want at the other end. A long move is the best possible prompt to be ruthless: walk every room, open the loft boxes, and be honest about what has not seen daylight since you moved in.

With a rough date in mind, get it booked with your removals firm as early as you can. Good crews and the right vehicles for a long haul get reserved well in advance, particularly in the busy spring and summer months. We're happy to hold a provisional date and firm it up once your contracts are exchanged. Around the same time, open a single folder, paper or digital, for everything to do with the move, and start a running list of who needs to know your new address.

This early phase is unglamorous but it is where the whole move is won. Decluttering now means less to pack, less to load, less to transport and less to unpack two hundred miles away, and that saving compounds at every single stage.

sections.h: Sorting the Admin and Address Changes
sections.body:
Long-distance moves involve more admin than local ones, simply because you are usually leaving a whole area behind rather than staying within reach of it. Set aside an evening to list everyone who holds your address: banks and building societies, your employer, HMRC, the DVLA, insurers, the schools, your GP and dentist, the vet, utility providers, and the dozens of subscriptions and accounts that quietly accumulate. Working through that list steadily over a few weeks beats a panicked scramble the night before.

Set up a postal redirection with Royal Mail to catch anything you miss; it is inexpensive peace of mind, and on a long move you cannot easily pop back for stray letters. Tell both councils about your move, the one you are leaving and the one you are heading to, so your council tax is correct at both ends. If you are moving far enough that you will change GP surgery, dentist and even your children's schools, start those registrations early, because new-area waiting lists can be longer than you expect.

It is also worth sorting your utilities for both properties. Take final meter readings at the old house and provide opening readings at the new one, and arrange for broadband at the destination well ahead, because installation slots in a new area can take weeks. Doing this admin in advance means you arrive to a home that actually works.

We cannot stress enough how much a long-distance move rewards a proper declutter. Every box you do not pack is a box you do not load, transport, unload and find a home for. Tackle it room by room over a few weekends rather than in one exhausting blitz, sorting into keep, donate, sell and recycle. Suffolk has plenty of charity shops and community groups glad of good furniture and household items, and selling the bigger pieces locally is far easier than dragging them across the country to a house where they may not fit.

Pay particular attention to the heavy and the bulky, because these are the items that cost the most to move a long way. That old sofa, the spare wardrobe, the exercise bike gathering dust; if they are not coming into the new home with purpose, the long move is the moment to let them go. The same goes for the garden, the shed and the garage, which are easy to forget until loading day and then become a frantic afternoon of decisions.

Be realistic about what fits at the destination too. If you are downsizing, or the new layout is very different, measure your largest pieces against the new rooms before you commit to moving them. We've carried many wardrobes hundreds of miles only for them to be too tall for the new bedroom, and a tape measure beforehand would have saved everyone the trouble.

sections.h: Packing Strategy for the Long Haul
sections.body:
Packing for a long-distance move needs more care than a local one, because your belongings will be on the road for hours rather than minutes. Start early with the rooms you use least, the loft, the spare room, out-of-season clothes, and leave the kitchen and bathroom essentials until last. Use sturdy double-walled boxes, do not overfill them, and keep the weight sensible so they can be lifted and stacked safely on a van that will be moving at speed for a long way.

Label every box clearly on the side, not just the top, with the room it belongs to and a note of the contents. For a long move this labelling earns its keep twice over, helping us load the van logically and helping you find things at the far end. Wrap breakables generously, fill any gaps in boxes so nothing rattles, and keep an inventory as you go. If packing feels like too much on top of everything else, our crews offer a full or partial packing service, and a professionally packed home travels a long distance far better.

Set aside an essentials box, or two, for the journey and your first night, and keep it with you in the car rather than on the van. Chargers, medication, a change of clothes, toiletries, the kettle and a few mugs, basic tools, and anything precious or irreplaceable should travel with you, so that even if the van arrives the next morning you have everything you need for night one.

The trickiest part of preparing for a long-distance move is that you are juggling two locations a long way apart, often within a chain of buyers and sellers. Keep in close contact with your solicitor and estate agent about the likely completion date, and share that information with us as soon as you have it so we can lock in the right crew and vehicles. The further the move, the less room there is to absorb last-minute changes, so the earlier everyone is working to the same date, the better.

Think carefully about the logistics of key handover. On a local move you can collect keys and have the van follow; on a long move the timing is tighter, and you may be travelling separately from your belongings. Agree with us in advance whether the van arrives the same day or the next morning, and plan your own journey and overnight arrangements around that. If there is any risk of a gap between completion dates, raise it early, because there are good solutions but they are far easier to arrange ahead of time.

Finally, prepare the destination as much as you can from afar. If you can get the keys, the meter readings and the parking situation sorted before the van arrives, the unload will be quick and calm. A little coordination between the two ends turns the most daunting kind of move into something genuinely manageable.

One of the smartest things you can do when preparing for a long-distance move is to consider storage early, even if you end up not needing it. Long moves are precisely where completion dates tend to slip and chains develop gaps, and having a plan ready means a wobble does not become a crisis. We've helped many Suffolk families bridge an awkward few days or weeks by keeping their belongings securely until the new home was ready, then delivering when the timing suited.

Storage is also worth planning in for the gentler reasons that often come with a big move. If you are downsizing, you may want time to decide what fits before everything travels hundreds of miles. If you are renovating the new place first, or relocating in stages, a secure store lets you move in phases rather than all at once. Mention the possibility at your survey so we can factor it into the overall plan and price, rather than scrambling to arrange it at the last minute.

Even if you only need it for a short spell, storage takes enormous pressure off a long-distance move. It means you can leave the old house on time without the new one being ready, and arrive when you are genuinely prepared, rather than living among boxes in an unfamiliar area while you wait for the rest of your life to catch up.

faqs: How early should I start preparing for a long-distance move?

We recommend beginning six to eight weeks ahead. A long move involves more admin, more decluttering and tighter coordination than a local one, and the right crew and vehicles get booked up early. Starting sooner gives you the date you want and a far calmer run-up.
What address changes are easy to forget on a long move?

The DVLA, your dentist and vet, the council at both ends, subscriptions and online accounts all slip people's minds. A Royal Mail redirection is good insurance, since you can't easily pop back for stray post once you've moved a long way. We suggest keeping a single running list and ticking it off over a few weeks.
Should I take everything with me or declutter first?

Declutter first, and be ruthless. Every item costs more to move the further it travels, so it makes no sense to transport things you don't want. Measure your largest furniture against the new rooms too, especially if you're downsizing, before committing to moving it hundreds of miles.
What if there's a gap between completion dates?

That's a common situation on long moves, and it's exactly what our storage solution is for. We can keep your belongings securely until the new home is ready and deliver when you are. Mention it at the survey so we can plan it in from the start rather than arranging it in a rush.
